speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to talk to you about when the transports left. What was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very sad. My husband never was home during the night before the transport-- always trying to figure out who went on transport. Were people going from his office? If they were, he tried to get them out. So he was never home that night. But sometimes, someone came inside to say goodbye to me. We never closed the door, not while we were living amongst the Jews. We didn't have to be afraid for them, and the Germans didn't come there at night.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I would be asleep, and I heard a knock on the bedroom door. And someone came in to say goodbye. And it was a loss.

speaker: interviewee
text: We tried so hard. And then you felt yourself a failure. Someone had to go again, and you couldn't prevent it. It was always hopeless. And then, say at about 8 o'clock, you saw all those people going past. When we were living inside the camp, they walk-- pass our bedrooms' windows. And the cattle wagons were in front of us. And you saw all those people pushed in the doors-- and old folk, people on [NON-ENGLISH]--

speaker: interviewer
text: On what?

speaker: interviewee
text: --little kids. What do you call it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Stretchers, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Stretchers-- brought to the train. And there the Germans were walking past, looking very self-proud, happy with themselves. Another train was going, and Berlin had asked them, for instance, for 1,000 people, and they make sure the 1,000 were there. And we tried to make sure that not 1,000, about a few less were going into there. So my husband even once took a boy away who was inside the train. That boy cried. And still, later on, he went, only around the court. That sounds strange. But I had, for instance, a girl working inside the house with me. Not that I needed help, but it was another one you could save from transport, so. And that was a few days before Nelly was born. And she came in the morning. And she said, Mrs. van As, don't get angry with me. I'm going on transport. I said, oh, come off it. You're not going on transport. And she said, yes. My brother and his wife had-- has-- have to go. And his wife is one of a set of twins. Her twin wants to go. And my sister and I thought, oh well, when those three are going, we go with them. With five young folk, oh, you can handle any-- anything-- situation. So with five, we are strong. I said, Leia, we are trying to get people out. Here, three are going on free will. Don't do it. And she said, yes. But we like to be with the five together. And they went. And when the train was standing there, Leia was standing in front of that door. She called out to me. Be strong. We will be back. Three days after she came there, I heard that they were gassed-- and all her relatives. We learned that later.

speaker: interviewee
text: So another time, a woman came in early in the morning. I was in the kitchen-- threw her arms around me. I said, I'm coming to say goodbye to you. I said why? Does my husband know?

speaker: interviewee
text: He wasn't working for my husband. He was the chauffeur. And she said, no. But an SS there or so had put her on transport. Don't let your husband try anything. It's too dangerous.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can I go to the girls-- say goodbye to the girls? So I said, yes. They are still asleep, but you can go into the bedroom. This woman had a child what was hiding-- in hiding. And she said, when my little boy comes in, please, can you take care that he stays here? Well, this woman went on transport.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what we later learned, there were kids sent by themselves. And there was a little girl crying. And this woman said, oh, come on. You stay with me. She was gassed with that little girl. Her husband survived. So if she hadn't been so kind to that little girl, maybe she had still been-- be alive. But her son survived. He is here in Sydney.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in hiding. And he was in hiding till the end. So when his father came back, he was looking for him, and found him. He visited us several times till he died. But the son is here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had another girl working for you, didn't you? You had two girls working for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I had several girls working for me. That was a little girl. She was a German girl. And she was about eight years old when that Kristallnacht came. You might-- may have heard of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So her parents said, it's too dangerous for her here. And he brought her to Amsterdam where a few aunts lived. And she stayed with those old aunts.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they were all picked up, the aunts and she. And she was in the camp. And she was about 15 years old. And she came to work with me-- one lovely little kid.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she would say, Mrs. van As, do you think my mother will still know me when she sees me? I said, yes, I did. Your mother knows exactly who you are when she sees you. A mother forget-- never forgets.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said, oh, I am so glad. This child was sent on transport. And she was sent to Bergen-Belsen-- what, in comparison, was one of the better camps. She survived the end of the war-- soon after, died from typhus. And before she died, she met a chap who was working with my husband. And she said, please, say hello for me to Mrs. van As. And then she died. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you mentioned--

speaker: interviewee
text: What kind of life has that child got-- eight years, torn from her parents, brought to old relatives, stayed there till she came in in camp. And-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned Theresienstadt. In January 1940, they sent nearly 5,000 to Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: '44, sorry-- January '44.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] That may have been an-- what they called an [NON-ENGLISH]. They swapped-- German soldiers were with Jewish people. And in that time, it was pretty good. Theresienstadt was one of the better camps. A lot of intellectuals had been sent there-- professors, doctors. And Professor Meyer went there, Professor Kiri, though went in. And of those people, a lot have survived. And they came back after the war. But I think after the first transportations, they came there, for instance. [NON-ENGLISH] came there too. And after a few days, they sent them on to Auschwitz. And, for instance, [NON-ENGLISH], what we learned later, was sent on a work camp. And the Germans who accompanied the work camps were told you go away with, say, 500 people, and we won't see more than 300 when you come back-- so over there, to shoot 300 during the line. And [NON-ENGLISH] was one of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And did you-- were you involved in the-- the Theresienstadt, the people that went? Did you have anything to do with those people when-- when they were chosen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not with the people-- the running of Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we know several people who went to Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, the ones that were chosen, was there any preference given, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: For instance, Professor Meyer was in-- wasn't Jewish anymore. He was, I think, Methodist, or he had-- he belonged to a Christian religion. Professor Kiri, though, wasn't a very well known doctor, specialist. Those people belonged to the Barneveld group. And the Barneveld group, first, were in a camp in Barneveld. And they were all kept behind a very special camp. Later on, they came to Westerbork. And from Westerbork, a lot of them have been going to Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Did you know, when they were being sent, where they were going? I mean, did you actually know these ones are going Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: These ones are going--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The ones to Theresienstadt went in a normal train. Auschwitz was cattle wagons-- and Auschwitz, Sobibor. They wrote it on the train-- Auschwitz, so many people. Sobibor.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the outside of the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: On each carriage, they--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have numbers on--

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so. I've seen it on the wagons, so.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many were in each--

speaker: interviewee
text: In each--

speaker: interviewer
text: --wagon?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 70. Can you imagine 70 people in-- in a cattle wagon? No fresh air-- a little window on the-- at the top-- no toilet, no nothing in it-- not in the [NON-ENGLISH]. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel when they left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very angry. For instance, once, I had an Jewish woman in my bedroom when the train was there. And [NON-ENGLISH], camp commander, was walking there. He always had a air of-- [SIGHS] And I said to her, I hope the war will be ended at the moment that we have got the train here in front of us. We go outside and tear them to bits. And this Jewish woman said to me, if you tried to harm [NON-ENGLISH], I will go and stand between you and [NON-ENGLISH]. I will defend him.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, you? How can you? And she said, Westerbork isn't bad for a camp. We live here better than, I think, in any other camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So. But I hated him. When you saw that train again, and you-- you couldn't help, and you couldn't do anything. And that chap was walking around with an air.

speaker: interviewer
text: [SIGHS] Were there Jewish collaborators there? Did-- did they have Jewish police?

speaker: interviewee
text: Later on they told us there was one [NON-ENGLISH], but I've never noticed any. No, I always thought the Jews are safe. Because the Jews aren't-- don't have to be afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: But everyone who's walking without a star, I would go to the office and say, hey. I've seen someone here. Can you find out who that is? You didn't trust anyone without a star. But the Jews, oh, they were all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: For instance, when my husband was picked up, Nelly was six weeks old. So that must have been at the end of August, '43. 6 o'clock in the morning, there were some, I think three marshals stayed in front of the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: Is your husband here? I said, yes. He's still asleep. Can you get him? We have to bring him to, I think, [NON-ENGLISH]. I said, why? Oh, we can't tell you why, but he has to come with us. We had just lent him a photo, or was it just a photo being made? So I said to my husband, you better take the photo. I won't see you back before the end of the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he went away. And perhaps an hour later, there comes-- trying-- came someone at the door. And he said, he wasn't in touch-- chapping. He was working in the kitchen.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, Mrs. van As, is that right? Has your husband been picked up this morning for-- what this is called? They call it [NON-ENGLISH]. No, black-- Black market?

speaker: interviewee
text: Black market. And I said, listen. If my husband is picked for black market, he'll be back today, because we never do anything black. Well, that evening, at about 6 o'clock, someone came and said, Mrs. van As, your husband's coming back. I said, hello. And we were living in-- on the same boulevard. And we see where all the people went to the-- to the-- cattle wagons. And I just walked over that road, and I would say, my husband's coming back. My husband's coming back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a party in the camp that night. It was marvelous. On all sides it, oh, have you-- have you heard that Mr. van As is coming back tonight? So. They had to ask him a lot of silly questions, but they couldn't find anything. So. What did they think he'd done?

speaker: interviewee
text: They always tried to find out something so they could give-- send him to a concentration camp as well. So they tried very hard. And well, he was, I think, taken three times in-- here-- taken away from home to be questioned and-- so you always had to be very careful not to be found out. And that's why you could never tell anybody what was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were your nerves?

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. No, most of the things we had went all right, so you felt quite safe. Another thing happened. My mother was always helping others. And one day, someone came and sold her some little, say, hangers, that should give-- bring her luck. What we later learned, that were Jewish signs, [NON-ENGLISH] or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I put it in my purse. If you can get luck, you should have it in your purse, so. And I lost it. I lost my purse.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to husband, well, this is not good, because in the purse was also a sign that I had delivered some grain. I bought them-- that from farmers around, and I-- I had brought it to the baker. And that gave me the right to buy so many loaves of bread. But you weren't allowed to do that. And I had it in my purse. So I thought, gee. Even such a little thing can hang me.

speaker: interviewee
text: After a few days, my husband said, well, your purse has been found. I said, oh good. Where? He said, you can get it at the commander's office.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, jeez. He said, yes. I've done so many awful things. Now you can go yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: So early in the morning, my mother was then inside in our house. I said, I go first to that commander and get my purse. My mother said, have something to eat first. But I was nervous. So I said, no. I'm not going to eat first. I'll eat when I come back. So when I came there, I said, in German-- in Dutch-- I never spoke a word of German to him. I heard that you found my purse.

speaker: interviewee
text: They said, I didn't find it. It was handed in to me. I said, oh, I'm glad! He said, in that purse is something. And then he found it, the little Jewish thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, what's that you're doing with [NON-ENGLISH]? I said, ah! My grandmother gave to me. Because I didn't dare to say my mother. My mother would had then be involved too. My grandmother gave it to me when she was very old. Is she still alive? I said, no, she died. That's why I keep it. It's a memory. He said, do you know what it means? I said, no-- just a little thing my grandmother gave to me. He said, well, I'm sorry. You can have your purse back-- not this one. And I came home, and I said, it will never bring him any luck.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER] But there were little things. You wouldn't think about it. And that can bring you into trouble. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Any other things that happened like that that you can think of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, a lot of things happened. Our eldest one was an very outgoing child. So if she saw someone crying, she went over.

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't cry. Would you like some grapes? And she would come home and say, mom, Henny wants some grapes. And I thought she'd eat it, so I gave her some grapes. Later on, people told me, no. She brought me grapes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she played with Jewish children. So one day, we were called. Our child was [NON-ENGLISH], friendly to Jews. And I had to go. I said, our child doesn't know what hate is. Our child doesn't know who a Jew is or who a German is. He said, well, I tell you one thing. You take care that the child doesn't get in contact with any Jews, or she will go where the Jews are going.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I bound-- bound to a long-- a long rope around her waist. I put her in the garden if she wanted to play. And it couldn't let her walk where she wanted to. But it nearly killed her, because she was playing in the yard and got her rope along on a branch of a tree. And she nearly hang herself. So my father said, that's too dangerous. I'll go for a walk every day with her. And he walked 10, 12 kilometers with a child to get her tired before she came to our place, that she wouldn't get in contact with Jewish children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Another time, she had picked some flowers. And we were called there. Yeah, your child had damaged his flowers. I said, well the child wouldn't do any harm to anyone.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had to pay, I think, 25 guilders or so. The child had damaged some flowers. So they always tried to get you. However small it was, they tried to get you. Do you think they tried to get you more than other people? Were they sort of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, of course. Because my husband was the only one who was working outside the German group. All the others were in-- in service of the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where the others Dutch people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Up till May '43. And then all the Dutch people went. Except some as theirs, they had signed for the [NON-ENGLISH] or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so why do you think your husband was allowed to stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Very easy! He was the one who wrote out the coupons. Without the coupons, no food-- not for the Germans either. And they had a very good life there, I tell you. The Jews had to bake, and cook, and make anything possible to have for them their parties. And what nowhere was for sale, they had it.

speaker: interviewee
text: One of parties was a-- a Christmas party.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that was in '42. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: German. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: A lot of screaming and-- roar! And the Jews, did they have any part in it-- in the preparations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but they actually didn't notice. For instance, when there was wedding on, they had their parties in their own little group. Or when there was a bar mitzvah on, they had their parties. But what the Germans had with the-- it wasn't a Christmas party. It's--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yule fest.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yule fest. That's-- there were a lot of-- of-- high prominents were there. We had to go that evening. We tried to get out of it. My husband said, no. He was sick. And-- and they said, if we didn't go, that was against, to say, the party. So we didn't think it was worthwhile to refuse. And in that time, we still had a lot of Dutch people working there. So-- and that was comical. They stood up and they said, oh, hail Hitler. And we said, [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH] Good evening. No one put up their hands. Yeah. None of the Dutch people?

speaker: interviewee
text: That were the Dutch people. [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened? Didn't-- did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were all gone by May '43, or they had to sign to be working with the Germans, or they had to get out. But he couldn't say that to my husband. Without my husband, they didn't have coupons, and they didn't get their food. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: Couldn't they make one of their own people into that-- get--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --have your husband's job?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. That was in Dutch department, and they had to-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] --yeah, take them in the theirs office. Germans couldn't do that. They couldn't say, we want, say, butter this week, or-- that was all done by the Dutch department. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My husband had to send in all the-- all the forms in to the head office. They didn't stay in Westerbork.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so without him--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it. And with him, everything was in the camp what was needed, not only for the Germans, but for the Jews. For instance, if an woman was pregnant, they got more milk and I think some eggs, or-- my husband wrote out those coupons. And then he would say, how many women are there? And he-- if they were pregnant or not. He had out so many women. And they got that extra milk-- milk, and-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So in that way, he was also able to keep them healthy.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. And he really looked after it, because he went to the hospital. And he was asking if they got what he thought that they should have. And it was given to them. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: That must have been very satisfying.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. He could help them a lot in that way.
